coredata.dc:title: Two birds with one stone: An innovative fluorescent cellulose polyacrylamide-hydrogel modified by born/nitrogen-doped carbon dots with sensitive visual sensing and superior extraction capacity toward Ag+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Houwen
Chen, Zewei
Wang, Changxing
Wang, Linfan
Wang, Xu
Shi, Yue
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Since the last decade, water pollution by silver ions (Ag+) has been a serious potential threat to environmental protection and an enormous research effort has been put into the detection and removal of environmental Ag+. Here, we report a novel fluorescent cellulose polyacrylamide-hydrogel (FCPH) modified by boron/nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) for sensitive visual detection and efficient extraction of Ag+. In addition, the introduction of BN-CDs in the cross-linking process promotes the formation of 3D network structures with more ionic adsorption sites in FCPH. On the one hand, the FCPH exhibits an excellent sensing performance in the range of 0–300 μM with a limit detection of 51 nM. On the other hand, the adsorption experiments confirm that the FCPH exhibits an excellent Ag+ extraction performance of over 251.4 mg‧g−1, and the adsorption results accord well with the pseudo-second-order model and the Langmuir model. Detailed mechanism characterizations reveal that the excellent adsorption capacity of FCPH is caused by the redox reaction and formation of metal complexes between the surface functional groups of BN-CDs and Ag+ in FCPH. Hence, this work fabricates a bifunctional hydrogel and provides a convenient “two birds with one stone” strategy for the visual sensing and extraction of Ag+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Born/nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent hydrogel
High efficiency Ag+ removal
Ag+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH
Optical properties and sensing of BN-CDs and FCPH
Adsorption capacity to Ag+ by FCPH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest. A table containing the abbreviations used in the manuscript has been included in the ESI (Table S8). Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: [details of any supplementary information available should be included here]. Figures (Figs. S1-S14). See DOI:

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of BN-CDs, illustrated by particle size statistics. (b) XRD patterns of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH. (c) FT-IR spectroscopy of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH. High resolution BN-CDs XPS spectra of C 1 s (d) and N 1 s (e). High-resolution FCPH XPS spectra of C 1 s (f) and N 1 s (g). (h) The images of FCPH before and after absorbing water. SEM images of CPH (i-j) and FCPH (k-l).

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of BN-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Linear fitting between F/F
0 and the concentration of Ag+ within a small range from 0 to 100 mg‧L−1. Under natural light (d) and ultraviolet lamp radiation (e) for synthesized FCPH after metal adsorption. (f) PL excitation and emission spectra of FCPH. (g) Fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs with gradually increased concentration of Ag+. (h) The relationship between fluorescence intensity (F/F
0 ) and Ag+ from 0 to 700 mg‧L−1.

(a) The comparison before and after Ag+ adsorption by FCPH. (b) Effects of pH on Ag+ adsorption (30 mg‧L−1 of Ag+ solution). (c) Comparison of Ag+ adsorption capacity of CPH and FCPH in 10–200 min (d) The adsorption ability of FCPH with different initial Ag+ concentrations at different temperatures. (e) The effect of different contact time on extraction capacity toward Ag+ (Insert: Fitting data of PSO model). (f) Intragranular diffusion model for Ag+ adsorption. (g) Ag+ adsorption fitting results about different initial concentrations using Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm models.

SEM (a) and EDX mapping (b-c) images of FCPH hydrogel after Ag+ adsorption. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs and the mixture of BN-CDs and Ag+. (e) FT-IR spectra of FCPH and FCPH with Ag+. (f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Ag+. High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s (g-h), N 1 s (i-j), and B 1 s (k-l).
Ag+ adsorption and detection mechanism of the novel FCPH hydrogel.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and applications of FCPH for Ag+.

head.ce:title: Two birds with one stone: An innovative fluorescent cellulose polyacrylamide-hydrogel modified by born/nitrogen-doped carbon dots with sensitive visual sensing and superior extraction capacity toward Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, MOE Key Laboratory for Nonequilibrium Synthesis and Modulation of Condensed Matter, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics, MOE Key Laboratory for Nonequilibrium Synthesis and Modulation of Condensed Matter, Xi'an Jiaotong University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Houwen Hu and Zewei Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the last decade, water pollution by silver ions (Ag+) has been a serious potential threat to environmental protection and an enormous research effort has been put into the detection and removal of environmental Ag+. Here, we report a novel fluorescent cellulose polyacrylamide-hydrogel (FCPH) modified by boron/nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) for sensitive visual detection and efficient extraction of Ag+. In addition, the introduction of BN-CDs in the cross-linking process promotes the formation of 3D network structures with more ionic adsorption sites in FCPH. On the one hand, the FCPH exhibits an excellent sensing performance in the range of 0–300 μM with a limit detection of 51 nM. On the other hand, the adsorption experiments confirm that the FCPH exhibits an excellent Ag+ extraction performance of over 251.4 mg‧g−1, and the adsorption results accord well with the pseudo-second-order model and the Langmuir model. Detailed mechanism characterizations reveal that the excellent adsorption capacity of FCPH is caused by the redox reaction and formation of metal complexes between the surface functional groups of BN-CDs and Ag+ in FCPH. Hence, this work fabricates a bifunctional hydrogel and provides a convenient “two birds with one stone” strategy for the visual sensing and extraction of Ag+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid development of global industrialization, silver is commonly applied in various industrial fields, including photography, pharmacies and electrical industries [1]. However, the abuse of silver has resulted in the release of approximately 2500 tons of silver into the environment each year, a portion of which enters surface water in the form of Ag+. Unfortunately, excess Ag+ in environmental water not merely spread in edible water and bioaccumulation in organisms, but will also pose a major threat to human health, such as organic damage and skin rash [2,3]. On the other hand, in considerations of environmental protection and resource recovery, Ag+ is an important precious metal resource that may be depleted due to overuse [4]. Apparently, the monitoring and extraction of Ag+ in water can reduce resource wastage while preserving the ecological environment. Thus, there is an urgent need to find suitable methods to achieve the “two birds with one stone” applications of rapid detection with high sensitivity and effective removal of Ag+ in the wastewater.

So far, diversified strategies have been used to remove Ag+ in aqueous solutions, such as ion-exchange, precipitation, reverse, osmosis and adsorption [5–9]. Among these methods, hydrogels have become particularly popular adsorbents because of their three-dimensional (3D) network structure and the abundant of hydroxyl and amino groups that can be coordinated with metals [10]. Nowadays, the efficient adsorptions of Cr6+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ have been achieved based on hydrogel materials [11–13]. However, the studies on the adsorption of Ag+ by hydrogels have rarely been reported. Furthermore, for purpose of enhancing the absorption performance of hydrogels, some methods such as metal nanomaterials intercalation and magnetic powder assist in improving the performance of hydrogels [14,15]. Inevitably, these methods increase the toxicity of the hydrogels, enlarge the cost of material preparation and complicate the ease of operation [16]. In contrast, carbon dots (CDs), as a promising nanoparticle, are known for their unique fluorescence and surface-rich functional group modifications properties. Besides, benefiting from the characteristics of tunable functional groups, CDs enable to bind with target metal ions firmly by means of surface modification [17]. Thus, by engineering a hydrogel with CDs, the adsorption capacity of heavy metals and targeted adsorption of target ions is enhanced. At the same time, the original fluorescence properties and sensing capabilities of the CDs are still preserved after embedding in the hydrogel structure. Therefore, in view of the “two birds with one stone” application in Ag+ adsorption and sensing, it is significant to develop CDs-based composites of specific sensitivity and high adsorption capacity hydrogels toward Ag+.

Against this background, a novel fluorescent cellulose polyacrylamide-hydrogel (FCPH) with strong Ag+ adsorption and sensitive detection abilities is prepared using polyacrylamide hydroxypropyl cellulose and boron/nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) ( Scheme 1). For the synthesized FCPH, the introduction of BN-CDs not only increases the adsorption sites of Ag+ in the 3D network, but also enhances the binding capacity of hydrogel toward Ag+, which achieves efficient sensing and adsorption of Ag+. Afterwards, through the systematical characteristics of Ag+ sensing ability and adsorption capacity, the as-prepared FCPH exhibits an excellent linear relationship with Ag+ in the range of 0–300 mg‧L−1, and its detection limit is determined to be 0.087 mg‧L−1 (51 nmol‧L−1). In adsorption experiments, the kinetic curves accord well with the pseudo-second order model, demonstrating the involvement of chemisorption. The adsorption isotherm measurements toward Ag+ show that the FCPH has a maximum uptake capacity of 251.4 mg‧g−1 in the water, which is much higher than the other reported Ag+ adsorbent materials. Furtherly, the Ag+ adsorption mechanism is proposed and investigated rationally based on experiment analysis. Overall, our approach provides a “two birds with one stone” strategy for simultaneous detection and removal of Ag+, relieving the pressures from various fields, such as environmental pollution detection, silver recovery, environmental remediation and water treatment.

Boric acid (99%), o-phenylenediamine (98%), hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC), polyacrylamide (PMA), ammonium sulfate (APS, 99%), bis-acrylamide (Bis, 99%) and N, N, N′, N′-Tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). AgNO3 (99.8%), Al(NO3)3 (98%), BaCl2 (99%), C4H6CoO4 (98%), CaCl2 (99%), MgCl2 (99%), Na2SO4 (99%), ZnCl2 (99.5%), K2CO3 (98%), CdCl2 (99%), LiCl (99%), Mn(NO3)2 (98%), KI (99%) and (NH4)2SO3 (92%) were available from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). These chemicals were analytically pure. Deionized water from the Millipore system was employed in the whole study.

Preparation of BN-CDs was conducted through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.3 g of boric acid and 0.5 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL water. The resulted solution dispersion was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then was heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the reacted solution was cooled down to indoor temperature naturally. After being filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove large particle impurities, the unreacted molecule was isolated using a 500 Da cellulose membrane. Finally, the purified solution was frozen at − 40 °C overnight and then lyophilized using a bench top freeze dryer (SCIENTZ-100 F, Scientz) under condenser temperature − 70 °C, pressure 10 Pa condition. The processing time was 48 h, and the BN-CDs solids were obtained for the further use.

Primarily, fully dissolved HPC (0.128 g) in water (10 mL) under ultrasonic to form HPC colloid. Then, prepared 2 mL of 0.58 g‧mL−1 BN-CDs solution and mixed it with HPC colloid to form BN-CD/HPC colloid. Subsequently, the BN-CD/HPC colloid was evenly coated on the surface of silicon wafer and freeze-dried to obtain BN-CD/HPC for further characterization.

PMA (1 g), APS (0.1 g) and Bis (0.05 g) were dissolved in water (10 mL), and then sonicated for 1 h. Subsequently, mixed the solution with HPC colloid (10 mL) to form CPH colloid via a water bath at 60 °C for 2 h. Then, in low temperature (0 °C) and nitrogen atmosphere, dropped the polymerization accelerator TEMDE (2 μL) for polymerization to form CPH. The formed CPH was soaked in water for 3 days with a frequent replenishment of water to remove all unreacted species. The freeze-dried CPH was prepared for the follow-up experiments.

Similarly, during the above experiments, the HPC colloid was replaced with BN-CDs/HPC colloid to obtain the FCPH. The FCPH was washed three times with deionized water to remove the unencapsulated BN-CDs and unreacted species. Finally, the fluorescent FCPH was then freeze-dried for the sensing and adsorption experiments.

Height distribution of BN-CDs was characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H-8100) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). The morphology and structure of the as prepared hydrogels were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800 SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX). UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies were monitored on Shimadzu UV-2550 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer and Hitachi FL7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer instrument, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD, XD-2X/M4600) was employed for microwave digestion. Chemical components of BN-CDs and hydrogels were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet Avatar 360) spectrophotometer from 400 to 4000 cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi). The concentrations of metal ions in the solution were determined using Agilent 725-ES ICP-OES (Agilent, USA).

The fluorescent FCPH was immersed in water samples containing Ag+ ions at different concentrations. Then the fluorescence spectra of the samples were recorded after magnetic stirring for 1 h. In order to perform interference experiments, several metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, K+, Cd2+ and Li+) were introduced instead of Ag+.

0.1 g FCPH and CPH were placed in 20 mL Ag+ solution at different initial concentrations. Placed the mixture for 800 min at different temperatures (299 K, 309 K and 319 K). The remaining concentration of Ag+ in the solution was detected on Agilent 725-ES ICP-OES. All the adsorption experiments were conducted thrice. Besides, the Eq. (1) was used to calculate the adsorption ability.
(1)
Q
Ag
+
= [
C
0
−
C
Ag
+
/ m ] × V
where
Q
Ag
+
(mg‧g−1) denotes the adsorption ability of FCPH to Ag+;
C
0
(mg‧L−1) is the initial Ag+ concentration;
C
Ag
+
(mg‧L−1) is the equal Ag+ concentration; V and m represents to the solution volume and the FCPH mass, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and height distribution of the synthesized BN-CDs are characterized by TEM and AFM. As presented in Fig. 1 (a), the synthesized BN-CDs have a spherical feature with the average particle size of 2.7 nm. Concomitantly, the AFM image shows that BN-CDs are uniform in height and dispersed well (Fig. S1). The calculated average height is about 1.2 nm, indicating that BN-CDs are composed of 3–4 layers of graphene planes [18]. The XRD patterns of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH are presented in Fig. 1 (b). BN-CDs show broad humps at approximately 21° in the XRD results, indicating an estimated interlayer spacing of 0.42 nm. Meanwhile, BN-CDs/HPC shows that the characteristic diffraction peaks of typical cellulose crystal structure are at 20° and 33° [19]. Besides, combined with the characteristic peak of BN-CDs at 21°, it is obvious that BN-CDs successfully combine with HPC. For FCPH, during the crosslinking process, the destruction of the crystal structure of BN-CDs and BN-CDs/CPH reduces the crystallinity of FCPH.

Subsequent, the functional groups of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH are analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 (c). For BN-CDs, two broad peaks at around 3244–3482 cm−1 are attributed to O-H and -NH stretching vibration [20]. The distinct absorption bands located at 1651 cm−1, 1602 cm−1 and 1476 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibrations of CO, CC and C-N, respectively [21,22]. The C-B stretching and B-OH bending vibration originating from BN-CDs appear at 1177 cm−1 and 1038 cm−1, respectively [23]. It indicates that boron atoms are successfully incorporated into BN-CDs. For BN-CDs/HPC, the FT-IR spectrum clearly shows the CO, CC, C-N, C-B and B-OH characteristic peaks of BN-CDs. Remarkably, the vibration wavenumber of C-N decreases from 1476 cm−1 to 1461 cm−1 [24]. Meanwhile, two broad peaks of BN-CDs at 3244–3482 cm−1 transform into a single vibrational band. It is owing to the formation of hydrogen bonds, which increases the rigidity of the chemical structure and hinders the bending vibration of the group (O-H/-NH), thus causing a blue-shift in the wavenumber of the chemical bond [25]. These indicate that nitrogen-related functional groups in BN-CDs are suspended in HPC by hydrogen bonding and further incorporated into hydrogels. Moreover, the abovementioned CO, C-N and B-OH characteristic bands of FCPH are observed, and these absorption bands produce distinct blue shifts of 1637 cm−1, 1440 cm−1 and 1018 cm−1, respectively, demonstrating that BN-CDs are immobilized in the hydrogel backbone via a hydrogen bond network.

Besides, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH are monitored and further compared for studying their chemical structure. Fig. S2 shows the full survey XPS spectra and percent atomic content of the BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH. It shows four major peaks belonging to C 1 s at 284 eV, N 1 s at 399 eV, O 1 s at 531 eV and B 1 s at 190 eV, respectively. In the deconvoluted C 1 s of BN-CDs, three peaks at 283.50 eV, 284.54 eV and 287.87 eV are ascribed to the C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and CO, respectively (Fig. 1 (d)) [26,27]. Compared with BN-CDs, the bond energy of the CO bond of FCPH is reduced in Fig. 1 (f). Previous research results show that the formation of hydrogen bond can influence the chemical bond energy, bond length and electron density [27]. Accordingly, the reduction of bond energy is speculated to be due to the generation of hydrogen bonds between the hydrogel and BN-CDs. Furthermore, the N 1 s peak (Fig. 1 (e)) of BN-CDs consists of three bands at 398.25 eV, 400.44 eV and 401.13 eV, which are attributed to pyridine N, amino N and graphitic N, respectively. In particular, the binding energy of amino N originating from BN-CDs/HPC is fitted at 400.02 eV, which is smaller than that of BN-CDs alone (Fig. S3) [28,29]. Therefore, it further reveals that the BN-CDs suspend on the HPC through hydrogen bonds, which is consistent with the above FT-IR analysis results. Besides, as shown in Fig. 1 (g), the integrated intensities of amino N peak for FCPH are larger than those for BN-CDs and BN-CDs/HPC. By measuring the high-resolution N 1 s spectra of CPH (Fig. S4), illustrating that the content increasing of amino N in FCPH is attributed to the abundant amide groups in the hydrogel raw material PAM. The deconvoluted high-resolution O 1 s spectra indicate that BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH all contain three components corresponding to CO, B-O and C-O (Fig. S5). The B 1 s peaks of BN-CDs, BN-CDs/HPC and FCPH are also deconvoluted into three contributions (B-C, B-N and B-O) (Fig. S6). Combined with the FT-IR and XPS results, they all point and confirm that the BN-CDs have been successfully incorporated into the FCPH via a physical hydrogen bond network, and the as-prepared FCPH inherits the unique optical properties of the BN-CDs.

Subsequently, the water absorption capacity and swelling studies of FCPH are tested. Considering the influence of BN-CDs concentration on the water absorption capacity of FCPH, different FCPH are prepared with BN-CDs concentration of 0.19 g‧mL−1, 0.39 g‧mL−1, 0.58 g‧mL−1 and 0.77 g‧mL−1. After immersion in water for 250 min, the FCPH prepared from BN-CDs with a concentration of 0.58 g‧mL−1 has the highest water adsorption capacity (Fig. S7). Therefore, the hydrogel with this preparation condition is selected for further studies. Besides, as presented in Fig. 1 (h), the longitudinal height of FCPH expands from 1.5 cm to about 3.2 cm after immersion in water for 2 h and the degree of swelling is calculated as 587.14%, indicating that FCPH has an excellent adsorption expansion capacity.

Afterwards, the structure of FCPH by EDX and SEM are further characterized. As shown in Fig. S8, the presence of homogeneous C, N and O elements in the FCPH can be seen from the EDX plots, which also indicates that the homogeneous distribution of chemical groups with metallic binding makes the adsorption sites homogeneous. Moreover, SEM is used to determine the morphology of the CPH (Fig. 1 (i-j)) and FCPH (Fig. 1 (k-l)). CPH shows a shallow 3D pore structure, and the thicker wall of hole reduces the adsorption sites, which seriously affects the metal adsorption capacity. However, FCPH exhibits a fluffy 3D porous network structure with significantly increased adsorption sites. The change of the structure from CPH to FCPH may be due to the electrostatic repulsion between the polymer chains of hydrogel, which causes by the negative groups (-COOH and -NH2) on the BN-CDs surface and hinders the aggregation of the polymer chains, thus forming a porous network structure interconnected pore [30]. Clearly, embedding BN-CDs in this network not only improves the structure of the hydrogel, but also facilitates enhanced sensing and adsorption capabilities of the hydrogel to ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and sensing of BN-CDs and FCPH
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence (PL) properties of BN-CDs are characterized thoroughly and scientifically. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of BN-CDs ( Fig. 2 (a)) shows a main peak at 207 nm and a shoulder peak at 235 nm, which are owing to the π-π* transition of CC conjugated double bond resulting from core of BN-CDs [21]. The peak at 289 nm is the excitonic absorption peak of carboxyl group from the n-π* transition of CO [31]. Besides, a weak broad peak at 421 nm corresponds to n-π* transition of C-N that originates from the emission of nitrogen-related functional groups at the edge of BN-CDs [32]. Under the optimal excitation wavelength of 410 nm, PL spectrum (Fig. 2 (a)) exhibits a strong emission peak situated at 563 nm. Moreover, BN-CDs exhibit remarkable excitation-independent PL behavior (Fig. 2 (b)), which stems from single surface states and the homogeneous microstructures [33]. Excitation-emission matrix (Fig. S9) shows a single fluorescence emission center, further indicating the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior. Furthermore, the photostability of BN-CDs is explored through pH, photobleaching and storage stability. As depicted in Fig. S10 (a), the fluorescence BN-CDs reaches a maximum at pH= 7 while quenches in acidic or alkaline environments. Besides, its fluorescence remains basically stable after long-term UV lamp irradiation (0–30 h) and initial long-term storage (0–30 day), which powerfully confirms the robust fluorescence property of the as-prepared BN-CDs (Fig. S10 (b-c)).

Subsequently, the sensing ability of BN-CDs for Ag+ is explored. The selectivity study is firstly carried out in the BN-CDs aqueous solution, involving an individual addition of 11 metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, K+, Cd2+ and Li+). It is obvious that Ag+ could induce strong fluorescence quenching, while other metal ions almost show no quenching (Fig. S11 (a-b)). Upon the illumination of UV lamp, the yellow fluorescence of BN-CDs is strongly extinguished in the Ag+ solution, while the bright original fluorescence remains after the addition of other ions (Fig. S12). Notably, after adding Ag+ in the BN-CDs solution, the solution color changes from transparent yellow to opaque dark brown, which suggests the existence of chelation between BN-CDs and Ag+. The fluorescence intensity toward Ag+ ions of different concentration are further explored to assess the detection capability. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs gradationally quenches with the increased concentrations of Ag+ from 0–500 mg‧L−1. The relationship between PL emission intensities of BN-CDs and Ag+ concentrations in the range from 0–100 mg‧L−1 can be found in Fig. 2 (c), and the detection limit of Ag+ is estimated to be 0.12 mg‧L−1 based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of blank signal (n = 15), s is the slope of the fitted curve), respectively. The detailed data have been summarized in Table S1.

Encouraged by the fluorescent sensing properties of BN-CDs, further implementations of fabricated hydrogels for Ag+ sensing have been performed. Fig. 2 (d) presents an actual photo of the FCPH after immersion in solutions of 11 metal ions for 1 h. Obviously, the FCPH color changes significantly after soaking in Ag⁺ metal ions, which is similar to the BN-CDs solution and indicates that metal ions have entered the FCPH structure and triggered chelation reaction. Moreover, under UV light irradiation, the fluorescence of FCPH is quenched by Ag⁺. Furtherly, solid-state sensing performance of FCPH for metal ions is explored. Fig. 2 (f) shows the PL excitation and emission spectra of the FCPH, which the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are located at 414 nm and 560 nm, respectively. Under the optimal excitation wavelength detection, as shown in Fig. 2 (g), the fluorescence intensity of FCPH decreases distinctly with the increase of Ag+ concentration (0–700 mg‧L−1), and a good linear relationship (Fig. 2 (h)) is observed between the Ag+ concentration of FCPH and the fluorescence quenching ratio (F/F
0 ) (0–300 mg‧L−1, R
2 =0.997), where F
0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity of FCPH without Ag+, and F represents the fluorescence intensity after adding Ag+. The calculated detection limit is 0.087 mg‧L−1 (Table S2). Besides, the inset photograph of Fig. 2 (h) shows the fluorescence of FCPH is gradually extinguished with the increase of Ag+ concentration. The above experimental results demonstrate that the as-prepared FCPH has an efficient metal sensing capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity to Ag+ by FCPH
ce:section.ce:para: The study on the adsorption performance of FCPH to Ag+ is shown in Fig. 3 (a). The FCPH is immersed in a transparent beaker containing 500 mL of water and 500 mg of AgNO3. After 60 min, the color of the light blue solution becomes significantly off-white and the hydrogel turns from yellow to black, confirming that FCPH has an excellent adsorption response behavior for Ag+. Accordingly, further detailed Ag+ adsorption experiments are carried out. Firstly, the effect of pH on Ag+ sorption is studied and the sorption process is investigated at 30 mg‧L−1 of Ag+ solution with pH range from 2 to 8. Fig. 3 (b) shows that the adsorption capacity of FCPH increases with increasing pH from 2 to 5 and then decreases with further increasing pH from 5 to 8, which is attributed to the protonation of FCPH and the positive charge leading to Ag+ repulsion at lower pH levels [24]. With the increase of pH, there is electrostatic interaction between FCPH and Ag+, which enhances the adsorption capacity. However, after reaching the weak alkalinity, the adsorption capacity decreases again due to Ag+ hydrolysis [34]. Therefore, the optimal pH condition is determined to be 5.0. Moreover, in order to confirm that the incorporation of BN-CDs improves the adsorption performance of hydrogels, the adsorption studies of Ag+ by FCPH and CPH are also compared. As presented in Fig. 3 (c), the adsorption capacity of FCPH is significantly higher than that of CPH with the increase of adsorption time in the 30 mg‧L−1 of Ag+ solution.

To further estimate the adsorption properties, spontaneity and energy changes in the adsorption process are explored at different temperatures. As illustrated in Fig. 3 (d), with the increase of temperature, the adsorption capacity exhibits an obvious increasing trend, indicating that an increase of temperature is conducive to the adsorption of Ag+. Besides, the variables of gibbs free energy (ΔG), enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS) in the adsorption system are calculated according to the thermodynamic Eqs. (2–5) [35].
(2)
K
c
=
Q
Ag
+
C
Ag
+
(3)
∆ G = − RT ln
K
c
(4)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ H
(5)
ln
K
c
=
∆ S
R
−
∆ H
RT
where the K
c (mL‧g−1) is the partition coefficient;
C
Ag
+
(mg‧g−1) and
Q
Ag
+
(mg‧L−1) represent the Ag+ concentration and the equilibrium concentration of Ag+, respectively; T (K) is the Kelvin temperature during the adsorption process and R (8.314 J‧mol−1‧K−1) is the gas constant. The corresponding detailed data is presented in the Table S3. ΔG< 0 can be seen as the temperature increases under above conditions, indicating that the adsorption is a spontaneous process. Meanwhile, the absolute value of ΔG gradually increases as the temperature increases, suggesting that heating is beneficial to the adsorption reaction, which is consistent with the experimental results. The calculated ΔH= +43.22 kJ‧mol−1 and ΔS= +149.3 J‧mol−1‧K−1 are both positive values, indicating that the Ag+ adsorption process is an endothermic and entropy increasing reaction.

As a major parameter during the adsorption process, the effects of contact time on the adsorption process of FCPH toward Ag+ are studied accurately. Adsorption kinetic data are usually fitted with pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) equations. Specifically, PFO is based on the Lagergern model which is used for explaining the physical adsorption process, whereas, PSO is mainly designed for chemisorption, usually through ion exchange and covalent bond chemisorption processes involving electron sharing or transfer between the adsorbate and the adsorbent. PFO and PSO are fitted with the following Eqs. (6–7), respectively [36].
(6)
Q
2
=
Q
1
1 − exp
−
k
1
t
(7)
Q
2
=
ht
1 +
k
2
Q
1
t
Where k
1 (min−1) and k
2 (g‧mg−1‧min−1) are rate constants of PFO and PSO models, respectively; t (min) is adsorption time; Q
1 and Q
2 (mg‧g−1) are equilibrium and real-time Ag+ adsorption amount, respectively; h is the initial adsorption rate constant. As shown in Fig. 3 (e), during the Ag+ adsorption process, Q
2 increases sharply in the first 100 min, then slows down and finally reaches equilibrium after 200 min due to the occupancy of adsorption sites. The kinetic linear fitting results are listed in Table S4. Obviously, the adsorption experimental data is fitted well to the PSO model (R
2 =0.986), which has a better linear fitting than PFO. Thus, the suitability of the PSO model for the absorption data reveals that the chemisorption process dominates the adsorption process of Ag+. In addition, to further study the adsorption process, the intraparticle diffusion is discussed by Weber-Morris intra-particle diffusion model (Eq. (8)) [37].
(8)
Q
2
=
k
p
t
0.5
+ C
where the k
p (mg‧g−1‧min−1/2) is the intraparticle diffusion rate constant, and C is the correlation constant of intraparticle diffusion. The fitting results shown in Fig. 3 (f) and Table S5 is composed of two parts, demonstrating the Ag+ adsorption process is governed by two adsorption stages. Therefore, the adsorption rate is not merely controlled by internal diffusion, but by various adsorption mechanisms that follow the internal diffusion process. To be specific, in the initial adsorption stage, the adsorption capacity increases with t
0.5 increasing, which is the instantaneous diffusion stage of Ag+ in the FCPH boundary layer. With the increase of contact time, the adsorption capacity still increases with the increase of t
0.5 . However, since the adsorbed Ag+ on the outer surface of FCPH gradually diffuses into the interior of the FCPH, the adsorption process is controlled by the intragranular diffusion, resulting in a decrease for slope and the adsorption rate. Eventually, the adsorption equilibrium is reached.

Additionally, detailed research about the effect of initial Ag+ concentration on adsorption is further explored. As shown in Fig. 3 (g), the adsorption capacity of FPCH gradually increases as the increase of the initial Ag+ concentration, and with the further increase of initial concentration, it finally stabilizes. Two adsorption isotherm models of Langmuir (Eq. (9)) and Freundlich (Eq. (10)) models are used to fit the equilibrium data [38].
(9)
Q
1
=
Q
m
K
L
C
Ag
+
1 +
K
L
C
Ag
+
(10)
Q
1
=
K
F
C
Ag
+
1
n
where Q
m (mg‧g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent. K
L and K
F are the constants of Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively, and 1/n is an empirical constant. According to the fitting results (Table S6), the Langmuir isotherm model fits the experimental data better than the Freundlich isotherm model by comparing the correlation coefficient (R
2 ), and demonstrates that the adsorption process of Ag+ by FCPH is a monolayer adsorption. Based on Langmuir model, the calculated maximum adsorption capacity is 251.4 mg‧g−1, which shows relatively high sorption capacity for Ag+ compared with the other adsorbents, confirming that the superior performance of the as-prepared FCPH for Ag+ adsorption (Table S7).

In order to explore the adsorption mechanism, the EDX-mapping, UV–vis, FT-IR and XPS spectra are conducted in the presence and absence of Ag+. As present in Fig. 4 (a), the cross-sectional SEM image of FCPH after Ag+ adsorption shows 3D porous network structure, indicating that Ag+ has a little effect on the physical structure of FCPH. Simultaneously, it can be seen from the EDX diagrams (Fig. 4 (b-c)) that C, O, N and Ag elements are dispersed homogenously in the FCPH, and further confirms the FCPH characteristic of uniform adsorption. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the BN-CDs in Fig. 4 (d) exhibit two new absorption bands appearing at 289 nm and 435 nm after addition of Ag+, while the original absorbance of CO and C-N bonds of BN-CDs is disappeared. It can be inferred that these new bands are triggered by charge transfer between CO and C-N forming ligands with Ag+ and the production of Ag+ complexes with BN-CDs with direct coordination to the N and O atoms [39]. Thus, we consider that fluorescence quenching of FCPH by Ag+ could be assigned to the chelation of BN-CDs with the Ag+, facilitating charge transfer from the excited state of the BN-CDs to Ag+. Fig. 4 (e) shows the FT-IR spectra of FCPH before and after adsorption. The stretching vibrations of O-H/-NH, CO, C-N and B-OH bonds have a significant blue shift, and the vibration intensities of all peaks reduce. These changes indicate that O-H/-NH, CO, C-N and B-OH groups with Ag+ form complexes through strong chelation [40].

Subsequently, XPS spectra of FCPH before and after Ag+ adsorption are also investigated. After the Ag+ adsorption, the full survey XPS spectra (Fig. S14) shows two new peaks appeared at 367.21 and 373.22 eV, which are related to Ag+. And the high-resolution Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 4 (f)) also consists of two peaks at 367.21 eV and 373.22 eV, belonging to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively, which is indicative of the existence of metallic Ag (Ag0) in the FCPH after adsorption [39]. Consequently, during the adsorption process, redox reaction occurs and Ag+ are reduced to Ag0. As displayed in Fig. 4 (g-h), after the adsorption of Ag+, the peak area of CO bond increases from 27.6% to 38.2%, while the peak area of C-O bond decreases from 18.15% to 8.7%, which is ascribed to the oxidization of BN-CDs groups from C-O to CO in the hydrogel. Notably, the spectral peak (531.59 eV) of CO after the uptake of Ag+ has shifted to lower values (530.91 eV), and its decrease in binding energy reflects the decrease in the electron density on the atom, which reveals that the electrons in the valence band of CO are possibly transferred, and the complexation are formed with Ag+. Additionally, in the spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 4 (i-j)), a significant intensity decreasing in the characteristic peak of amino N is observed after adsorption, indicating that the amino N also participates in the adsorption process, which is consistent with the above FT-IR spectrum. Meanwhile, B 1 s spectrum of FCPH before and after adsorption are shown in Fig. 4 (k-l), the proportion of B-O bond at 192.01 eV has decreased. Previous literature reported that there is a strong electron transfer ability between boron and silver [41]. Thus, we speculate that electron transfer occurs between B atom and Ag+ in the adsorption process, which promotes the adsorption of Ag+.

Based on the abovementioned results, the Ag+ adsorption and detection mechanism of the novel FCPH hydrogel can be as follows ( Fig. 5). When FCPH is immersed in Ag+ solution, Ag+ gradually diffuses from the surface to the interior through the 3D porous network structure of these FCPHs. Due to the presence of BN-CDs, the surface amino groups and boron-related groups promote the electron transfer between FCPH and Ag+, which is conducive to capturing more Ag+. Furthermore, with the increase of contact time, Ag+ quickly transfer from the surface of FCPH to the internal area. Meanwhile, due to the existence of a large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, the hydrogel can be used as an electron donor after being combined with Ag+, which causes Ag+ to oxidize C-O to CO, while the chemical valence of Ag+ is reduced to Ag0. In addition, some Ag+ combines with carboxyl groups to form a non-fluorescent complexation, resulting in fluorescence quenching of FCPH. Thus, FCPH exhibits excellent performance adsorption capability and ultrasensitive sensing capability for Ag+.

To sum up, BN-CDs are synthesized hydrothermally and successfully introduced into hydrogels to form the bifunctional FCPH through hydrogen bond network for the visual sensing and extraction of Ag+. The introduction of BN-CDs not only endows the hydrogel with the visual sensing ability, but also improves the extraction capacity of the hydrogel to Ag+. Thus, FCPH exhibits a highly sensitive fluorescence response toward Ag+ over an analytical linear range from 0 to 300 mg‧L−1 with a limit detection of 0.087 mg‧L−1 (51 nmol‧L−1). Besides, the adsorption experiments confirm that FCPH exhibits an excellent Ag+ extraction capacity of over 251.4 mg‧g−1. In all, this work supplies an innovative “two birds with one stone” approach for the design and preparation of functionalized fluorescent hydrogel that can be simultaneously applied in both detection and removal of Ag+, paving a way for silver recovery, environmental remediation and water treatment.

Wang Xu: Data curation. Chen Da: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Shi Yue: Validation, Supervision. Chen Zewei: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Hu Houwen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Wang Linfan: Validation, Data curation. Wang Changxing: Project administration, Data curation.
